did_this_paper_use_ai_to_obtain_results_.details: The paper uses a shallow Convolutional Neural Network (CNN) architecture named SkinNet-8 to classify skin cancer (malignant vs. benign) using dermoscopic images from the ISIC 2020 dataset. The study involves deep learning model training, preprocessing, and evaluation for automated skin cancer diagnosis.

is_this_paper_a_review_paper_or_a_resear.details: The paper presents original experimental work involving the development of a novel shallow CNN architecture (SkinNet-8) for skin cancer classification. It details specific methodology including dataset preprocessing (ISIC 2020), data balancing techniques, model architecture, and reports original performance metrics (98.81% accuracy). Although published in a conference proceedings (ECCE 2023), it contains original results and is not a review.

title: SkinNet-8: An Efficient CNN Architecture for Classifying Skin Cancer on an Imbalanced Dataset
journal: 2023 International Conference on Electrical, Computer and Communication Engineering (ECCE)
brief_summary: This study addresses the challenge of classifying malignant and benign skin cancer using dermoscopic images from the highly imbalanced ISIC 2020 dataset. The authors propose SkinNet-8, a shallow Convolutional Neural Network architecture designed to reduce computational complexity while maintaining high accuracy. To handle data imbalance and noise, the research employs morphological closing for preprocessing, along with random undersampling and photometric augmentation for data balancing. The model's performance was evaluated using metrics such as accuracy, precision, recall, and F1-score. The proposed SkinNet-8 achieved a test accuracy of 98.81% and an F1-score of 98.82%, outperforming several transfer learning models in both accuracy and processing time.

lesion_types_studied.details: The study classifies dermoscopic images from the ISIC 2020 dataset into two binary classes: malignant and benign lesions, specifically focusing on melanoma. The dataset is described as highly imbalanced with a paucity of malignant class images.

all_task_types.target: Binary classification of dermoscopic images into malignant or benign skin cancer classes.
all_task_types.details: The paper explicitly states the objective is to 'classify skin cancer' and 'distinguish between malignant and benign Melanoma'. The model uses a softmax activation function for binary classification. The target classes are 'malignant' and 'benign' based on the ISIC 2020 dataset labels.

datasets_used.name: ISIC 2020
datasets_used.description: A dermoscopic image dataset provided by the International Skin Imaging Collaboration (ISIC). It contains images collected from 200 patients, labeled based on histopathology and expert opinion as benign or malignant lesions. The images are in JPG format and contain noise such as hairlines, gel bubbles, and ink marks.

use.training: Used for training the SkinNet-8 model after preprocessing (morphological closing to remove noise) and data balancing (random undersampling of benign class and photometric augmentation of malignant class).
use.testing: Used for testing the model, achieving 98.81% accuracy.

metrics_used_to_evaluate_results: Miss Rate or False Negative Rate (FNR)

statistical_methods_used.purpose: To represent the statistical significance of the experiment and validate the reliable performance of the proposed model.
To highly significantly validate the model's prediction capability.
To determine performance metrics including precision, recall, specificity, F1-score, FPR, FNR, and FDR.
To demonstrate the model's statistical significance and error magnitude.
To demonstrate the model's statistical significance and error magnitude.

hypothesis_testing.interpretation: The paper did not report formal hypothesis testing (e.g., t-tests, ANOVA, p-values) for statistical significance between models. It relied on performance metrics (accuracy, F1-score, etc.) and statistical measures like Kappa Coefficient (KC) and Matthews Correlation Coefficient (MCC) to validate model performance, but did not conduct formal hypothesis tests comparing the proposed model against others.

metric: Miss Rate or False Negative Rate (FNR)
ISIC 2020.notes: The proposed SkinNet-8 model outperformed all compared transfer learning models in terms of accuracy and processing time. The study highlights that SkinNet-8 achieved the highest accuracy (98.81%) and lowest loss (0.07%) among the evaluated models on the ISIC 2020 dataset.

block_two.description: Contains two convolutional layers and a single max-pooling layer

block_three.description: Contains two convolutional layers and a single max-pooling layer

architecture_details.post_processing: Flattening of feature maps to a 1D vector

architecture_used.approximate_parameters: Not explicitly reported, but described as having 'fewer trainable parameters' compared to deep CNNs and transfer learning models.

preprocessing_techniques: Resizing input images to a resolution of 1080 x 720 pixels
Morphological closing (dilation followed by erosion) using a cross-shaped kernel with size (20, 20) to remove hairline noise
Random undersampling of the majority (benign) class to reduce sample size to 12542
Photometric augmentation of the minority (malignant) class involving alterations in brightness, contrast, sharpening, and color to increase image count to 11680

data_augmentation_techniques.details: Applied to the majority class (benign) to reduce the sample size from 32,542 to 12,542 images.

Applied to the minority class (malignant) to increase the image count from 584 to 11,680. Techniques included alterations in brightness, contrast, sharpening, and color. The process involved modifying pixel intensity using the formula OutputPixel(x) = SourcePixel(x) + α, where α is a constant determining brightness changes.

conclusions: The proposed SkinNet-8 shallow CNN architecture achieved a test accuracy of 98.81% and an F1-score of 98.82% on the imbalanced ISIC 2020 dataset, outperforming transfer learning models such as VGG16, VGG19, InceptionV3, MobileNet, and ResNet50.
SkinNet-8 demonstrated superior computational efficiency, requiring less processing time per epoch (220-230 seconds) compared to deeper transfer learning models, while maintaining high accuracy.

The study confirms that preprocessing techniques (morphological closing) and data balancing strategies (random undersampling and photometric augmentation) are critical for handling noise and class imbalance in dermoscopic images.
Statistical validation using the Kappa Coefficient (98.67%) and Matthews Correlation Coefficient (99.28%) confirmed the reliability and significance of the model's predictions.
The shallow architecture of SkinNet-8 reduces computational complexity and parameter count compared to deep CNNs, making it a viable option for efficient skin cancer classification.

The authors acknowledge that the dataset contains numerous noises and artifacts, and that previous studies often failed to adequately address preprocessing and data balancing, which contributed to their lower performance compared to the proposed method.
important_remarks: The study relies on a single, highly imbalanced dataset (ISIC 2020) with no external validation, limiting generalizability to other populations or imaging devices.

Data balancing was achieved via random undersampling of the majority class (benign) and photometric augmentation of the minority class (malignant), which may introduce bias or overfitting to augmented features rather than true biological variation.
The paper does not report formal statistical hypothesis testing (e.g., p-values) to validate the significance of performance differences between SkinNet-8 and transfer learning models.

The dataset contains significant noise (hairlines, gel bubbles, ink marks) which was addressed via morphological closing; however, the effectiveness of this preprocessing on diverse real-world clinical images is not verified.
The model was trained on images resized to 1080x720 pixels, which may not reflect standard clinical input resolutions or preserve fine-grained diagnostic features.

The authors acknowledge that dermatologists identify melanoma with less than 80% accuracy in clinical settings, yet the model reports >98% accuracy, raising questions about potential data leakage or overly optimistic evaluation metrics compared to human baselines.
The study is published in a conference proceeding (ECCE 2023) rather than a peer-reviewed journal, which may imply less rigorous scrutiny of methodology and results.
No information is provided regarding the computational resources (e.g., GPU type) or specific hardware used for training, making reproducibility of processing times difficult.
The model architecture (SkinNet-8) is proprietary to the authors and not open-sourced in the provided text, hindering independent verification.
